title: spoolctl v0.1: reliability core
description:
spoolctl is a daemonless local job queue CLI: shell jobs are enqueued into a
SQLite database and executed by symmetric peer worker processes, with no
broker, daemon, or third-party dependency (Python 3.10+ stdlib only, macOS and
Linux). v0.1 delivers the reliability core: atomic job claiming, crash
recovery that never double-executes, automatic retry with exponential backoff,
dead-lettering, per-job timeouts with process-group kill, durable per-attempt
output capture, and an agent-first CLI contract (universal JSON envelope,
documented exit codes, machine-readable capabilities).

Verbs: add, work, status, retry, output, capabilities.

Hard guarantees this epic must ship: (1) a job is never executed by two live
workers at once — absolute, even under stalled heartbeats; (2) at-least-once —
a SIGKILLed worker's job is eventually reclaimed and retried by any other
worker; (3) failed jobs retry up to max_retries times after the initial
attempt (default 3), then land in a durable dead state.
acceptance_criteria:
- All six verbs implemented per their child issues and green in CI on
  ubuntu-latest and macos-latest.
- Concurrency/crash suite (no-double-execution, SIGKILL recovery,
  no-false-positive-reap, timeout, process-group kill) passes flake-free.
- Envelope and capabilities outputs pinned by golden-file tests.
- Installable via pip and as a single stdlib-only file that runs `--help`.
close_reason: v0.1 reliability core shipped: six verbs (add/work/status/retry/output/capabilities) per child specs; CI green 4/4 on ubuntu+macos x py3.10/3.13; concurrency suite (6 scenarios) 10x flake-free locally and green in CI; envelope-per-verb + capabilities goldens pinned; pip console script verified in a venv and single-file artifact smoke-tested in CI from a foreign cwd. Hard guarantees each carry a dedicated regression test: mutual exclusion (16-worker race + SIGSTOP no-false-reap), at-least-once SIGKILL recovery, max_retries+1 then dead. Implementation commits 3f3cdda..HEAD on main.
agent_context: {"constraints":["Python 3.10+ standard library only, including tests - a third-party import anywhere is a defect","All SQLite access goes through store.py; no other module touches the database","Never hold a DB transaction while a child process runs","Invariant priority: never-execute-a-job-on-two-live-workers is absolute and outranks prompt recovery; any inconclusive liveness check resolves to assume-alive, do-not-reap","Every verb supports --json and emits the universal envelope (ok, tool_version, data, meta, warnings, commands, errors - all keys always present, arrays never omitted)","Exit codes only from the published dictionary: 0 success, 1 user-input-error, 2 safety-block, 3 tool-environment-error, 4 transient/retryable, 5 conflict; empty results are exit 0","Persisted timestamps are time.time() epoch floats; time.monotonic() only for in-process deadlines","No ANSI color output in v0.1"],"conventions":["data payloads are deterministic: sorted iteration, no timestamps in data (call facts live in meta)","Intentional contract changes re-pin golden files and get a changelog entry"],"rejected":["Lease-expiry-only reclaim of running jobs: can double-execute a stalled-but-alive worker's job","Reaping on heartbeat staleness alone: same double-execution hazard; staleness only nominates candidates","Joining argv tokens into a shell string: silently corrupts quoted arguments; the -c form is the only shell path"]}

title: Scaffold package, models, and single-file build
description:
Fresh repository needs the package skeleton for a stdlib-only Python 3.10+
CLI that distributes two ways: a normal pip package and a single-file
artifact (the zero-install path for sandboxed environments). All later work
hangs off this layout.
design:
Package `spoolctl/` with four modules: `store.py` (all SQLite access),
`cli.py` (argparse dispatch, envelope, rendering, exit codes), `worker.py`
(claim/execute loop, heartbeat, reaper, timeout), `models.py` (dataclasses
Job/Attempt; state constants queued|running|done|failed|dead; tunables
BACKOFF_BASE=2, BACKOFF_CAP=60, HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL=5,
REAP_THRESHOLD=max(4*HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL, 30); CONTRACT_VERSION="1"; the
exit-code and error-code dictionaries). pyproject.toml with console script
`spoolctl = spoolctl.cli:main`, no dependencies. A build script (e.g.
zipapp or module stitcher) that emits a runnable single-file `spoolctl.py`.
acceptance_criteria:
- `python -m spoolctl --help` and the console script both work.
- Build script emits a single file that runs `--help` with only a Python
  3.10+ interpreter present.
- A test asserts no imports outside the standard library anywhere in the
  package.
close_reason: Package skeleton, models.py (states/tunables/exit+error dicts), pyproject console script, single-file builder via embedded-source meta-path finder, stdlib-only AST guard test. python -m, console script, and single-file artifact all verified running --help. Commit 3f3cdda.

title: retry verb
description:
Manual requeue of a failed or dead job with a fresh retry budget (operator
intent is "give it a real second chance"). Must never race a live worker:
retrying a running job is refused unless explicitly forced, because the
only safe automatic path for a running row is the reaper's confirmed-dead
check.
design:
On dead|failed: state=queued, attempts=0, next_run_at=now, locks cleared,
event 'retried'; return {job_id, state}. On queued or done: CONFLICT exit 5
(done's remediation shows the re-add command). Unknown id: NOT_FOUND exit
1. On running: SAFETY_BLOCK exit 2; the error names the safe alternative
(wait for automatic recovery once the owning worker is confirmed dead) and
the forcing form. `retry --force <id>` (operator recovery for a
hung-but-alive worker): under BEGIN IMMEDIATE re-check state='running',
mark the attempt abandoned, requeue; if the row changed underneath,
CONFLICT exit 5.
acceptance_criteria:
- Each source state maps to its documented exit code (dead/failed → 0,
  queued/done → 5, running → 2, unknown → 1).
- retry then work re-runs the job with a fresh budget (attempts reset).
- --force on a running row requeues it, and the displaced worker's
  subsequent recording affects zero rows.
- A forced retry that races a state change exits 5.
close_reason: retry verb with full documented exit-code map, --force with in-transaction re-check (raced -> 5) and displaced-recording discard verified. Discovery fixed in claim_next: attempt_no = MAX(attempt_no)+1 per job, since retry's attempts=0 reset made jobs.attempts+1 collide with UNIQUE(job_id,attempt_no); regression asserted (rerun is attempt 2). Commit e954776.

title: output verb
description:
Retrieve captured stdout/stderr for any attempt of a job, at any time
after execution, from any process — including one that did not submit the
job. Output must survive retries (per-attempt files) and round-trip binary
content exactly.
design:
Default: latest attempt, both streams, with per-stream headers. --attempt N
selects a historical attempt. --stream stdout|stderr|both. --raw writes
bytes straight to sys.stdout.buffer with no headers (requires a single
--stream). --json returns paths, sizes, and UTF-8-with-replacement previews
rather than raw bytes. Job exists but has no attempts yet: exit 0 with
data {attempts: []} and warning NO_ATTEMPTS_YET — not an error. Unknown
id: NOT_FOUND exit 1.
acceptance_criteria:
- --raw round-trips binary output byte-exactly.
- Historical attempts remain retrievable after later retries.
- Headers identify job, attempt, and stream in the default mode.
- No-attempts-yet case exits 0 with the warning; unknown id exits 1.
close_reason: output verb complete: binary --raw round-trip asserted via real subprocess, historical attempts after retry retrievable, headers carry job/attempt/stream, NO_ATTEMPTS_YET warning path, raw+json and raw+both rejected with corrected commands. Commit 5aa35f5.

title: capabilities verb
description:
The machine contract agents read once: what verbs and flags exist, what
exit codes mean, which env vars apply. If it isn't in capabilities, it
shouldn't exist — undocumented surface is a bug.
design:
data: verbs (flags with types and defaults, brief data-schema summaries),
exit_codes with retryable booleans, error_codes, env (SPOOLCTL_DB,
SPOOLCTL_TEST_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL, SPOOLCTL_TEST_REAP_THRESHOLD),
contract_version. Generated from the same tables the argparse parser is
built from — no hand-maintained shadow copy. Output golden-pinned;
intentional contract changes re-pin the golden file and are called out in
the changelog. `--help` stays terse (core in under 30 lines) with an
AGENT/AUTOMATION footer pointing here.
acceptance_criteria:
- A test asserts parity: every flag the parser accepts appears in
  capabilities and vice versa.
- Golden-file test fails the build on any drift.
- Envelope-compliant; deterministic output.
close_reason: capabilities verb generated from the live parser (no shadow copy); golden pinned at tests/golden/capabilities.json with drift-failing test and re-pin instructions in the assertion message; bidirectional flag-parity test; env vars and exit-code retryability documented. Commit c79a7f8.

title: Unit and contract test suite
description:
The CLI contract and state machine are the product; both get pinned so
regressions fail the build rather than surprising agents in the field.
design:
stdlib unittest + tempfile; temporary databases throughout; no shared
state. Store/state-machine units: schema idempotency; claim transitions;
backoff values 2/4/8 capped at 60; dead after exactly max_retries+1 failed
executions; manual retry resets the budget; guarded recording affects zero
rows after reclaim. Contract tests: envelope golden files per verb (seven
keys, empty-array discipline); capabilities golden; an exit-code assertion
for every documented path (empty work --once → 0, retry-on-running → 2,
unknown id → 1, DB busy → 4, raced force → 5); did-you-mean on a typo'd
verb and flag; argv-vs--c fidelity pair (`add -- echo 'hello && goodbye'`
echoes literally; `add -c 'echo a && echo b'` runs the shell); determinism
(two identical calls, identical data_hash); end-to-end
add → work → output → retry flow.
acceptance_criteria:
- Every case listed in the design exists and passes locally.
- Golden files are committed and drift fails the suite.
- Suite runs with the standard library only.
close_reason: Every design-listed case now exists: envelope goldens per verb (committed, drift-failing, REPIN flow documented in assertion), DB-busy exit 4 via held BEGIN IMMEDIATE + shrunk busy_timeout, backoff 2/4/8/16/32/60/60, executed shell-vs-argv pair, end-to-end lifecycle. Full suite 136 tests, stdlib only (AST guard enforces). Commit 5c5c1be.

title: Concurrency and crash-recovery suite
description:
Release-gating proof of the headline guarantees. Every reliability claim
this tool makes publicly is an assertion in this suite; it is the reason
to trust the tool.
design:
Real worker subprocesses against temporary databases, short
SPOOLCTL_TEST_* thresholds. Scenarios: (1) no double execution — N jobs,
K workers with K substantially greater than N, each job appends to a
per-job marker file; assert every marker written exactly once; (2) SIGKILL
recovery — kill a worker mid-job, start a fresh worker, assert the job is
reaped and completes; (3) false-positive-reap regression — SIGSTOP a live
worker past the reap threshold, assert its job is NOT reclaimed while the
pid lives, then SIGKILL and assert reaping proceeds; (4) timeout cascade —
always-failing timeout job reaches dead with backoff-consistent wall time;
(5) process-group kill — grandchild is gone after timeout; (6) guarded
recording — a displaced worker's record-after-reclaim affects zero rows.
acceptance_criteria:
- All six scenarios automated and green on macOS and Linux.
- Suite completes in under ~2 minutes using test-threshold env overrides.
- Ten consecutive runs pass without flakes.
close_reason: All six release-gating scenarios automated with real worker subprocesses and SPOOLCTL_TEST_* thresholds; suite runs ~26s, 10 consecutive runs passed flake-free. SIGKILL-recovery assertion encodes literal at-least-once (orphaned child may duplicate side effects; attempts accounting exact). Commit e688987.

title: CI workflow: ubuntu and macos matrix
description:
Process-group signal semantics and ps output are the platform-sensitive
surface; both target OSes must gate every merge, not be checked manually
after the fact.
design:
GitHub Actions workflow: matrix over ubuntu-latest and macos-latest, Python
3.10 and current stable. Steps: unit/contract suite, concurrency suite,
single-file build smoke test (artifact runs --help). Concurrency suite runs
with test-threshold env overrides to stay fast.
acceptance_criteria:
- Workflow green on both OSes on the default branch.
- A failing test on either OS blocks merge.
- Single-file artifact built and smoke-tested in CI.
close_reason: CI matrix (ubuntu/macos x 3.10/3.13): full suite + single-file smoke test from foreign cwd; green 4/4 on main (run 29076589xxx series). Two real portability fixes shaken out: dash printf lacks \xHH (tests now emit binary via python -c) and a slow-runner flake in the guarded-recording test (marker file replaces fixed sleep). Branch protection now requires all four checks (admins exempt). Commits 54a2782..HEAD.

title: store.py: schema, pragmas, DB path resolution
description:
All coordination between concurrent processes happens through one SQLite
file; correctness depends on every access going through a single storage
layer with the right pragmas and a deterministic database location.
design:
Schema (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, idempotent):

jobs: id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT; argv_json TEXT NOT NULL (JSON array);
state TEXT NOT NULL CHECK IN (queued,running,done,failed,dead); attempts
INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 (count of FAILED executions); max_retries INTEGER
NOT NULL DEFAULT 3 (retries AFTER the initial attempt; dead once attempts >
max_retries); timeout_seconds INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 300; created_at REAL
NOT NULL; next_run_at REAL NOT NULL; locked_by TEXT; locked_pid INTEGER;
locked_at REAL; heartbeat_at REAL; started_at REAL; finished_at REAL;
last_exit_code INTEGER; last_error TEXT. Index idx_jobs_claimable on
(state, next_run_at).

attempts: id PK; job_id REFERENCES jobs(id); attempt_no (1-based, UNIQUE
with job_id); worker_id TEXT NOT NULL; worker_pid INTEGER NOT NULL; state
CHECK IN (running,succeeded,failed,timed_out,abandoned); started_at;
finished_at; exit_code; stdout_path TEXT NOT NULL; stderr_path TEXT NOT
NULL; error TEXT.

job_events (append-only audit): id PK; job_id; at REAL; event TEXT
(added|claimed|succeeded|failed|timed_out|dead|retried|reaped); worker_id;
detail.

meta: key TEXT PK, value TEXT — stores schema_version.

Every connection sets: journal_mode=WAL, synchronous=NORMAL,
busy_timeout=5000, foreign_keys=ON. All timestamps are time.time() epoch
floats.

Path resolution precedence: --db flag > SPOOLCTL_DB env > default
./.spoolctl/queue.db relative to CWD. Canonicalize with os.path.realpath
before opening (macOS /var vs /private/var must resolve to one queue).
Auto-create the parent directory. A schema_version newer than this build
understands is a hard error: tool-environment-error, exit 3, message says
to upgrade spoolctl.
acceptance_criteria:
- Schema init is idempotent (double init no error).
- Pragmas verifiable on an open connection.
- Precedence flag > env > default covered by tests.
- Two path spellings of one file (symlinked/`/var` style) resolve to the
  same DB in tests.
- Newer schema_version exits 3 with a clear message.
close_reason: store.py: idempotent schema (jobs/attempts/job_events/meta), WAL+NORMAL+busy_timeout+foreign_keys pragmas on every connection, resolve_db_path with flag>env>default and realpath canonicalization, SchemaTooNewError gate, row converters and event helper. 16 tests green. Commit c198816.

title: CLI framework: envelope, errors, exit codes
description:
The primary users of this CLI are AI agents. Every verb must speak one
machine contract: a universal JSON envelope, a documented exit-code
dictionary, and errors that teach the corrected invocation. Building this
once, before any verb, prevents per-verb drift.
design:
Envelope (every `--json` response, success or failure): top-level keys ok,
tool_version, data, meta, warnings, commands, errors — all seven always
present, arrays empty rather than omitted. meta always carries request_id,
ts_iso, elapsed_ms, contract_version, data_hash (sha256 of canonicalized
data). data is deterministic: sorted iteration, no timestamps (call facts
live only in meta). Errors: objects {code, message, remediation,
did_you_mean?, exit_code} in errors[], ok=false, data=null, and
errors[0].message mirrored to stderr. Failure always produces stderr output
plus a non-zero exit — no silent-fail path.

Exit-code dictionary: 0 success (including empty results); 1
user-input-error; 2 safety-block; 3 tool-environment-error; 4
transient-failure (retryable, e.g. DB busy past busy_timeout); 5 conflict.
Never overload non-zero for "ran fine, no results".

Error codes: INVALID_INPUT, MISSING_REQUIRED, UNKNOWN_FLAG, UNKNOWN_COMMAND,
NOT_FOUND, CONFLICT, SAFETY_BLOCK, LOCKED, TIMEOUT, INTERNAL. UNKNOWN_FLAG /
UNKNOWN_COMMAND populate did_you_mean via Levenshtein distance <= 1 against
the parser's own verb/flag tables (never a hand-maintained list).

Without --json: concise human text on stdout, diagnostics on stderr, no ANSI
color anywhere in v0.1. DB busy surfaces as LOCKED, exit 4, remediation
"retry after a few seconds".
acceptance_criteria:
- Envelope helper emits all seven keys with empty-array discipline.
- Identical data payloads produce identical data_hash across calls.
- A typo'd verb and a typo'd flag each yield did_you_mean plus a
  copy-pasteable corrected command.
- Every failure path writes stderr text and exits non-zero.
- Exit codes come only from the published dictionary.
close_reason: Envelope helper (7 keys, empty-array discipline, sha256 data_hash over canonical JSON), CliError->exit mapping, UNKNOWN_COMMAND/UNKNOWN_FLAG did-you-mean from parser tables with corrected-command remediation, LOCKED/exit-4 and SchemaTooNew/exit-3 translation, stderr+nonzero on every failure path. 13 framework tests. Commit follows this close in log order.

title: add verb: argv default, -c shell string
description:
Enqueue a job. Two input modes by design: default argv form stores exactly
the tokens the caller's shell delivered (fidelity- and injection-safe for
programmatic submitters — no second shell ever reinterprets arguments), and
an explicit -c form for callers who want shell semantics (pipes,
redirection, &&, globbing).
design:
`spoolctl add [--] <argv...>`: everything after `add` (past optional `--`)
stored verbatim as a JSON array; executed later with shell=False.
`spoolctl add -c '<string>'`: exactly one string argument, stored as the
array ["sh", "-c", "<string>"] — one storage format and one execution path
for both modes. Validation before touching the DB: no command at all →
MISSING_REQUIRED exit 1 showing both corrected forms; -c combined with
positional args → INVALID_INPUT exit 1; --timeout must be > 0 (default
300); --max-retries >= 0 (default 3). Insert row state=queued,
next_run_at=now, log event 'added'. Returns data {job_id, state}. Human
output: `Added job <id>`.
acceptance_criteria:
- `add -- echo 'hello && goodbye'` stores a 3-token argv and later echoes
  the literal string (no shell interpretation).
- `add -c 'echo a && echo b'` stores ["sh","-c","echo a && echo b"].
- Empty command, -c plus positionals, timeout <= 0 each exit 1 with a
  corrected command in the error.
- --json returns job_id in the envelope; human mode prints `Added job N`.
close_reason: add verb complete: hybrid argv/-c semantics per ruling R1, single storage format (JSON array), validation before DB open (verified by test asserting no DB file), added event logged, envelope + human output. 14 grammar tests through the real CLI surface. Commit 94a4857.

title: Atomic claim and guarded result recording
description:
Mutual exclusion across independent worker processes is the hard invariant
of the whole tool. It rests on two storage operations: claiming (must be
atomic under concurrency) and result recording (must refuse to clobber a
row that was reclaimed out from under a stalled worker).
design:
Claim, one BEGIN IMMEDIATE transaction: SELECT the oldest eligible row
(state='queued' AND next_run_at <= now, ORDER BY next_run_at, id LIMIT 1);
UPDATE it to running setting locked_by, locked_pid, locked_at, heartbeat_at,
started_at; INSERT the attempts row (attempt_no = jobs.attempts + 1,
state='running', output paths preassigned); log event 'claimed'; COMMIT.
BEGIN IMMEDIATE takes SQLite's write lock before the read, so concurrent
claimants serialize and the loser re-reads a taken row. No transaction is
ever held while a child process runs.

Recording (success and failure alike) guards on ownership: UPDATE jobs ...
WHERE id=? AND state='running' AND locked_by=? AND locked_pid=?; the
attempts row by primary key WHERE state='running'. Zero rows affected means
the job was reclaimed (reaped or force-retried): log a warning to stderr
and discard the stale result. Success: state=done, locks cleared,
finished_at, last_exit_code=0, event 'succeeded'. Failure: attempts+1;
backoff delay = min(60, 2 * 2**(attempts-1)) seconds; attempts <=
max_retries → state=queued with next_run_at=now+delay; attempts >
max_retries → state=dead; both written in the same transaction as the
failure itself, events 'failed'/'timed_out' then 'dead' when applicable.
acceptance_criteria:
- Two sequential claims never return the same job.
- Guarded update affects zero rows after the row is reassigned, and the
  stale worker's result is discarded with a stderr warning.
- Failure recording writes requeue-or-dead atomically in one transaction.
- Backoff sequence is 2s, 4s, 8s; a job is dead after exactly
  max_retries + 1 total failed executions.
close_reason: claim_next + record_success + record_failure in store.py with ownership guards (id, state, locked_by, locked_pid). 12-thread disjoint-claim test, guarded-discard tests (incl. pid-only mismatch), backoff 2/4/8 asserted, dead after exactly max_retries+1, reaped/timed_out/dead events. 57 tests green. Commit 6509827.

title: Worker execution: spawn, output capture, timeout
description:
Execute a claimed job as a child process with durable output capture and a
hard timeout that kills the job's entire process tree, not just its shell.
design:
subprocess.Popen(argv, stdout=<file>, stderr=<file>,
start_new_session=True). Output files at
<db_dir>/output/<job_id>/<attempt_no>/{stdout,stderr}; directories created
at spawn; earlier attempts' files are never clobbered by retries. Streams
are redirected straight to files — no pipes, so no draining threads, no
memory cap, no deadlock. Timeout enforced on the monotonic clock: on
expiry os.killpg(pgid, SIGTERM), 5 seconds grace, then os.killpg(pgid,
SIGKILL); recorded as attempt state timed_out with error "timed out after
Ns", same retry accounting as any failure. Spawn failures
(FileNotFoundError, PermissionError) are recorded as an immediate failed
attempt through the normal retry path; the worker loop survives them.
acceptance_criteria:
- Captured bytes are exact per attempt and preserved across retries.
- A `sleep 5` job with timeout 1 is killed within a small margin and
  recorded timed_out.
- A job spawning a grandchild (`sh -c 'sleep 100 & wait'`) leaves no
  surviving grandchild after timeout.
- A nonexistent binary produces a failed attempt and the worker continues.
close_reason: execute_attempt in worker.py: redirect-to-file capture (byte-exact incl. binary, per-attempt files preserved across retries), monotonic-deadline timeout with SIGTERM/grace/SIGKILL process-group kill (grandchild test), spawn failure as normal failed attempt. 64 tests green. Commit cbbb505.

title: Heartbeat and confirmed-dead reaper
description:
A SIGKILLed worker runs no cleanup, so its running job must be reclaimed by
peers — but reclaiming a job whose worker is still alive would violate the
never-two-workers invariant, which is absolute. Heartbeat staleness may
only nominate candidates; only positive confirmation that the owning
process is dead may reap. Every inconclusive check resolves to "assume
alive". The accepted cost: a live-but-permanently-hung worker's job is
never auto-reaped and waits for an operator.
design:
While executing, a background thread updates jobs.heartbeat_at every
HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL (default 5s) with a plain UPDATE — lost updates are
harmless. Before each claim, every worker runs a reap pass: SELECT running
rows with heartbeat_at < now - REAP_THRESHOLD (default max(4*interval,
30s)) as candidates. Per candidate, outside any transaction, confirm death
of locked_pid: (1) os.kill(pid, 0) raising ProcessLookupError → confirmed
dead; (2) otherwise guard pid reuse with `ps -p <pid> -o command=` (stdlib
subprocess; ps exists on macOS and Linux): a command line that is not a
spoolctl worker → confirmed dead (recycled pid); looks like a worker,
PermissionError, or ps inconclusive → treat as alive, skip. Confirmed-dead
rows reap under BEGIN IMMEDIATE with a re-check (state='running' AND
locked_pid=? AND heartbeat still stale), mark the attempt abandoned (error
"worker died"), then run the normal failure/backoff/dead path logging event
'reaped'. Env overrides for tests: SPOOLCTL_TEST_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL,
SPOOLCTL_TEST_REAP_THRESHOLD (documented in capabilities).
acceptance_criteria:
- A worker SIGKILLed mid-job has its job reaped and re-run by the next
  worker's reap pass.
- A live worker SIGSTOPped past REAP_THRESHOLD is NOT reaped while its pid
  is alive; after SIGKILL it is reaped normally (regression test for the
  false-positive-reap hazard).
- Inconclusive ps output results in no reap.
- Reaped jobs carry an abandoned attempt row and a 'reaped' event.
close_reason: Heartbeat thread (own connection, ownership-guarded UPDATE), is_worker_pid_dead with kill(0) + ps pid-reuse guard (every inconclusive branch returns alive), reap_pass + store.reap with BEGIN IMMEDIATE re-check. SIGSTOP-no-reap regression test in place (r02#1), plus recycled-pid, ps-failure, budget-exhaustion-to-dead cases. 77 tests green. Commit 337d07e.

title: work verb: loop, --once, signal handling
description:
The worker entrypoint. Workers are symmetric peers: any number may run
against one database with no coordination beyond SQLite, and crash
recovery is built into every worker's claim cycle.
design:
Loop: reap pass → claim → execute → record → repeat; sleep --poll-interval
(default 1.0s) when idle. --once: at most one job, then exit 0 whether or
not one ran — data.claimed reports which (empty queue is not an error).
--worker-id defaults to hostname-pid. SIGINT/SIGTERM: finish the in-flight
job, record it, exit 0; a second signal kills the job's process group
first. SIGKILL is deliberately unhandled — the reaper is the recovery
path. Loop mode writes nothing to stdout (per-job log lines to stderr);
--once honors --json with one envelope.
acceptance_criteria:
- `work --once` on an empty queue exits 0 with claimed=false in the
  envelope.
- `work --once` executes exactly one job end to end.
- First SIGTERM finishes the running job and exits 0; a second SIGTERM
  kills the child process group.
- Loop mode keeps stdout empty.
close_reason: process_one + work_loop in worker.py, cmd_work in cli.py. Real-subprocess tests: --once end-to-end (exactly one job), empty-queue claimed=false exit 0, loop stdout silence, first-signal-finishes-job, second-signal-group-kill (<10s, job requeued), worker-id recording, stale-result stderr warning path wired. 86 tests green. Commit fdb1978.

title: status verb
description:
One-look queue health for humans and agents: counts by state and the
recent dead jobs that need attention.
design:
data: {counts: {queued, running, done, failed, dead} — zero-valued states
included; recent_dead: up to --limit (default 10) entries {id, command
(truncated), attempts, last_error, finished_at, stdout_path, stderr_path},
sorted finished_at descending then id}. Pure SELECTs (WAL readers never
block workers). Always exit 0 — an empty or absent queue is a valid empty
result (the DB is created on first use).
acceptance_criteria:
- Counts correct, including explicit zeros for empty states.
- --limit honored; ordering deterministic.
- Exit 0 against a fresh directory with no prior database.
- Envelope output golden-pinned.

title: Add idempotency keys
description:
Retried submitters can enqueue duplicate work after an agent or process crash.
`add --key` should make blind resubmission safe while a matching job is still
active, without imposing a lifetime uniqueness invariant on terminal jobs.
design:
Add `spoolctl add --key K`. Normalize the key with `K.strip()`, store the
normalized key, require 1 to 256 printable characters after normalization, and
reject non-printable characters using Python `str.isprintable()`.

Inside the existing `add_job` `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction, look up active
rows with the same key in states `queued` or `running`, ordered by `id ASC`,
and return the oldest active row on a hit without touching the row or writing
an event. Terminal rows do not block key reuse. `retry` does not consult keys.
acceptance_criteria:
- `add --key K --json` inserts a fresh queued job with `deduplicated: false`.
- A second active `add --key K --json` returns the existing job id, existing state, and `deduplicated: true`.
- Dedup hits do not insert jobs, touch rows, or append events.
- Terminal jobs do not block key reuse.
- Retrying a terminal job does not consult or enforce idempotency keys.
- Keys are stripped before storage and lookup; `"  K  "` deduplicates against `"K"`.
- Empty, overlong, and non-printable keys fail through the real CLI parser path.
- Concurrent same-key submissions produce exactly one active row and all callers receive that row's id.
- The add data schema and conformance tests cover `deduplicated`.

title: Add tags and notes
description:
Jobs need handoff metadata so a later agent can understand why work was queued
and find related work across sessions. Tags and notes should be immutable
submit-time metadata, visible in detailed output and filterable in list.
design:
Add repeatable `add --tag KEY=VALUE`, `add --note STRING`, and repeatable
`list --tag KEY` / `list --tag KEY=VALUE`. Tag keys must match
`[A-Za-z0-9_.:-]+`, be non-empty, be unique within one add invocation, and be
at most 128 characters. Values may be empty and are at most 1024 characters.
Jobs may have at most 16 tags. Notes are free text up to 10000 characters.

Store tags as sorted JSON object text in `tags_json`. Evaluate list tag
predicates in Python, not SQLite JSON paths. When `--tag` is present, SQL may
apply state filtering and newest-first ordering, but must not apply the final
limit before tag filtering; scan newest-first and stop after `--limit`
matching rows.

- `add --tag` and `add --note` persist metadata and expose it in `show --json` and `list --json`.
- Human `show` prints idempotency key, tags, and note when present.

- Malformed tags, duplicate keys, too many tags, overlong keys, overlong values, and overlong notes fail through the real CLI path.
- `list --tag KEY` filters by existence and `list --tag KEY=VALUE` filters by exact value.
- Repeated `list --tag` predicates use AND semantics and compose with `--state`.
- `list --tag ... --limit N` returns the newest N matching jobs, not filtered results from the newest N total jobs.
- Tag keys containing `.`, `:`, and `-` round-trip and filter correctly.
- `retry` preserves key, tags, and note.
- List and show data schemas and conformance tests cover the new metadata fields.

title: Add events stream
description:
Agents and shell users need to consume the durable job event ledger without
polling many individual jobs. The `events` verb should expose one-shot reads,
long-poll reads, and a raw NDJSON tail over the existing `job_events` table.
design:
Add `spoolctl events` with `--job ID`, `--since-id N`, `--limit N`, `--wait`,
`--wait-timeout SECONDS`, `--follow`, `--poll-interval SECONDS`, `--db`, and
`--json`.

One-shot and `--wait` are envelope mode. Data is `{count, events}` where each
event is `{id, job_id, at, event, worker_id, detail}`. Metadata includes
`meta.pagination: {cursor, first_id}`; `--wait` also includes
`meta.wait: {waited_ms, reason}`. Cursor must never rewind. Use a `limit + 1`
fetch to distinguish truncated results from exactly-limit results. If the scan
reaches the matching end, cursor is `max(since_id, ledger_high_water)`; if
limit-truncated, cursor is `max returned id`. `first_id` is the minimum
retained event id for the stream predicate or null.

`events --follow --json` is raw mode, not frames mode: one event record per
line, no envelope, no control channel, no trailer, flushed after each line.
Mid-stream failures go to stderr with non-zero exit. SIGINT, SIGTERM, and
broken pipe exit cleanly. Pre-stream validation failures emit ordinary
envelope errors before the first record.
acceptance_criteria:
- One-shot `events --json` returns ascending events, count, and `meta.pagination`.
- `--since-id`, `--job`, and `--limit` work together without existence checks for `--job`.
- Empty one-shot results are success and return a non-rewinding cursor.
- Exactly-limit result sets are not misreported as truncated; limit-plus-one behavior is tested.
- `--wait` returns records when available and timeout success with empty events when quiet.
- `--wait` returns `meta.wait.reason` as `records_available` or `timeout`.
- `--wait --follow` and `--follow --limit` fail with `INVALID_INPUT`.
- `--follow --json` emits raw NDJSON event records only, flushes per line, and never emits control frames.
- Follow can start from now by default or from an explicit `--since-id`.
- Follow tolerates prune-induced id gaps without duplicates or crashes.
- Capabilities declare envelope and raw modes for events, including raw bare-record semantics.
- The events data schema, optional meta schemas, raw event record schema, and conformance tests cover all surfaces.
notes:
2026-07-15:
COMPLETED:
- Implemented events one-shot envelope mode with --since-id, --job predicate, --limit, ascending rows, and meta.pagination cursor/first_id.
- Implemented non-rewinding cursor semantics, including limit+1 truncation detection and filtered-stream cursor advancement to global high-water.
- Implemented --wait long-poll envelope mode with --wait-timeout, --poll-interval, records_available/timeout meta.wait reasons, and success on quiet timeout.
- Implemented --follow raw tail mode; --follow --json emits bare NDJSON event records only, flushes each line, defaults to current high-water, and exits cleanly on SIGINT/SIGTERM/BrokenPipe.
- Declared events raw mode in capabilities and added events payload schema/conformance/golden coverage.

title: spoolctl v0.2: operational completeness
description:
spoolctl v0.1 shipped the reliability core: six verbs (add, work, status,
retry, output, capabilities), atomic claiming, confirmed-dead reaping,
backoff, dead-lettering, timeouts with process-group kill, per-attempt
output capture, and the agent-first CLI contract. Day-2 operation still
lacks table stakes: you cannot enumerate jobs, inspect one job's history,
cancel work, block on completion, run a worker to queue-exhaustion for
cron/CI, or reclaim disk from old jobs and their captured output.

v0.2 adds five verbs — list, show, cancel, wait, prune — and one worker
mode, work --drain. It introduces a `canceled` terminal job state (which
forces the project's first schema migration, since job states are enforced
by a SQLite CHECK constraint) and a new exit code 6 for "the tool call
succeeded but an awaited job ended non-success".

Hard constraint carried over from v0.1: a job is never executed by two live
workers at once, absolutely — cancellation of a running job must not
introduce any cross-process signal path that could kill an innocent
process or bypass the ownership model.
acceptance_criteria:
- list, show, cancel, wait, prune, and work --drain implemented per their
  child issues; all speak the seven-key JSON envelope and appear in
  capabilities --json.
- Schema migrates v1 -> v2 automatically and safely under concurrency.
- New concurrency tests (cancel race, kill delivery, drain settling, wait
  through worker SIGKILL, prune safety) pass flake-free alongside the v0.1
  suite: 10 consecutive clean local runs and green CI on ubuntu/macos x
  Python 3.10/3.13.
- Envelope goldens exist for every new verb; capabilities golden re-pinned.

title: Schema v2: canceled state and the migration mechanism
description:
The cancel verb needs a `canceled` terminal state on jobs and attempts, but
both state columns are enforced by SQLite CHECK constraints, which cannot
be altered in place — adding an enum member means rebuilding the table.
This is the project's first schema migration; the mechanism built here
(versioned, concurrency-safe, automatic on connect) is what every future
schema change will reuse, so it must be done properly rather than minimally.
design:
Schema changes (SCHEMA_VERSION 1 -> 2):
- jobs.state CHECK gains 'canceled' (full set: queued, running, done,
  failed, dead, canceled).
- attempts.state CHECK gains 'canceled' (full set: running, succeeded,
  failed, timed_out, abandoned, canceled).
- New index idx_jobs_finished ON jobs (state, finished_at) — serves prune
  matching and recency queries.

Migration procedure, in store._ensure_schema when the stored
schema_version is 1:
1. BEGIN IMMEDIATE (write lock; concurrent migrators serialize).
2. Re-read schema_version inside the transaction; if already 2, COMMIT and
   return — the loser of a migration race becomes a no-op.
3. Rebuild jobs and attempts via create-new/INSERT SELECT/drop/rename
   (ids copied verbatim), create idx_jobs_finished, set schema_version=2,
   COMMIT.
- PRAGMA foreign_keys=OFF for the migrating connection during the rebuild,
  re-enable after, and run PRAGMA foreign_key_check to verify.
- Bump SCHEMA_VERSION to 2 in models.py.
- An older binary opening a v2 file already fails cleanly via the existing
  schema-too-new error (exit 3, "upgrade spoolctl"); no new code needed,
  but assert it in a test. The migration is a one-way door.
acceptance_criteria:
- Fixture test: create a populated v1 database from a frozen copy of the
  v1 schema SQL (jobs in every state, attempts, events), open it with the
  new code: all rows and ids preserved, schema_version=2, a canceled row
  is insertable in both tables, PRAGMA foreign_key_check is clean, and
  MAX(id) sequencing still advances correctly for new inserts.
- Race test: two processes connect to the same v1 database concurrently;
  both succeed, exactly one migrates, data intact.
- Entire existing test suite stays green on freshly created v2 databases.
close_reason: Schema v2 shipped in commit 4d85162: canceled added to both CHECK sets, idx_jobs_finished created, SCHEMA_VERSION=2, automatic v1->v2 migration in store (BEGIN IMMEDIATE, in-txn version re-read so race loser no-ops, table rebuild with ids and AUTOINCREMENT sequence preserved, foreign_key_check verified). Acceptance covered by tests/test_migration.py: frozen-v1 fixture upgrade, two-process race (exactly one rebuilds), id sequencing past deleted rows; full suite 143 green.

title: Contract additions: exit code 6, canceled enums, v0.2.0

- Additive-contract policy stated in capabilities data: under

  breaking change to an existing verb's data shape or exit mapping is what

Re-pin the capabilities and status goldens (SPOOLCTL_REPIN=1 mechanism).

- capabilities --json shows exit code 6 with the ok:true exception note,
  the additive policy, and canceled wherever states are enumerated.
- status on a fresh database reports canceled: 0 alongside the other five
  counts.
- Goldens re-pinned; full suite green.
close_reason: Shipped in commit 2d190bb: TOOL_VERSION 0.2.0, EXIT_JOB_FAILURE=6 with the documented ok:true/empty-errors exception, JOB_EVENT_TYPES incl. canceled, capabilities gains job_states/attempt_states/events enumerations and the additive contract_policy string, status zero-fills canceled, all goldens re-pinned. New tests assert the exit-6 note, canceled enumeration, and policy; suite 145 green.

title: list verb: enumerate jobs with state filters
description:
There is no way to see what is in the queue: status shows only counts and
recent dead jobs. Operators and agents need to enumerate jobs, filtered by
state, with machine-readable full-fidelity output.
design:
spoolctl list [--state CSV] [--limit N] [--db PATH] [--json]
- --state: comma-separated subset of the six job states; an unknown value
  is INVALID_INPUT (exit 1) with did_you_mean suggested from the state
  list via the existing Levenshtein<=1 helper.
- --limit: default 50; 0 means unlimited.
- Order: newest first (ORDER BY id DESC).
- data: {count, jobs: [{id, argv, state, attempts, max_retries,
  timeout_seconds, created_at, started_at, finished_at, next_run_at,
  last_exit_code, last_error}]}. argv is the full JSON array — fidelity in
  data; truncation is for humans only.
- Human output: one line per job, "#<id>  <state>  attempts=<n>  <cmd>"
  with the command truncated to 80 chars for display.
- Pure SELECT (WAL reader, never blocks workers). Valid input always
  exits 0; an empty result is {count: 0, jobs: []}.
- New store.list_jobs(conn, states, limit); new subparser + handler +
  verb-summary entry; envelope golden.
acceptance_criteria:
- Filter, limit, and ordering behavior asserted; bad state value exits 1
  with a did_you_mean; empty queue exits 0 with count 0.
- Two identical calls produce identical data_hash (data determinism).
- Golden pinned; capabilities lists the verb with its flags.
close_reason: Shipped in commit d20115e: store.list_jobs (pure SELECT, newest-first, IN-filter, limit 0=unlimited) + list subparser/handler/verb summary. Grammar tested through the real surface in tests/test_list.py (CSV states, whitespace, empty token, unknown state with did_you_mean, negative/zero limit), determinism via data_hash equality, envelope-list-empty golden pinned, capabilities re-pinned. Suite 161 green; smoke-tested via python -m spoolctl.

title: show verb: single-job detail with attempts and events
description:
Debugging one job today takes stitching together status, output, and raw
DB access. show gives the complete picture of a single job: current row,
every attempt with its outcome and output paths, and the append-only event
trail (added/claimed/failed/reaped/retried/...).
design:
spoolctl show <id> [--db PATH] [--json]
- data: {job: {full jobs row including argv, lock columns, timestamps},
  attempts: [{attempt_no, state, worker_id, worker_pid, started_at,
  finished_at, exit_code, error, stdout_path, stderr_path}] ordered by
  attempt_no, events: [{at, event, worker_id, detail}] ordered by event id}.
- Reuses store.get_job and store.get_attempts; adds
  store.get_events(conn, job_id).
- Human output: a detail block — one job header line, one line per
  attempt, one line per event.
- Unknown id: NOT_FOUND, exit 1. Integer-parse failure of the id argument
  follows the existing job-id argument handling (INVALID_INPUT, exit 1).
acceptance_criteria:
- A job that failed, backed off, and was retried shows the full attempt
  history and event sequence.
- Unknown id exits 1 with remediation; golden pinned.
close_reason: Shipped in commit (see git log: show verb). store.get_events added; cmd_show returns job/attempts/events with sorted keys; human detail block. tests/test_show.py covers the fail->dead->retry->succeed arc (attempt history + exact event sequence), lock-column visibility on running jobs, and id grammar (unknown NOT_FOUND, non-integer INVALID_INPUT, missing MISSING_REQUIRED). envelope-show-not-found golden pinned, capabilities re-pinned. Suite 168 green; smoke-tested against a real dead job.

title: cancel verb with owner-side kill delivery
description:
Queued jobs cannot be withdrawn and running jobs cannot be stopped except
by killing the worker. cancel must fix both without weakening the core
invariant (never two executors; only proven-safe kills). The constraint
that shapes the design: jobs.locked_pid records the worker's pid, not the
child's — the child's process group is known only to the owning worker. A
cross-process killpg from the cancel process would need new child-pid
plumbing plus a pid-reuse guard, and would break the single-owner kill
discipline. Instead, cancellation is delivered through the heartbeat
ownership guard the worker already runs: flip the row, and the owner
notices within one heartbeat and kills its own child.
design:
CLI: spoolctl cancel <id> [--running] [--db PATH] [--json]

store.cancel_job(conn, job_id, allow_running, now) — one BEGIN IMMEDIATE,
outcome strings in the style of the existing retry_job:
- queued -> canceled: finished_at=now, event 'canceled'. Outcome ok.
- running, allow_running=False: outcome running_unforced -> CLI
  SAFETY_BLOCK exit 2; the error names both the do-nothing alternative and
  "cancel --running <id>".
- running, allow_running=True: re-check state='running' in the
  transaction, flip to canceled, clear lock columns, finished_at=now, mark
  the running attempts row canceled (WHERE job_id=? AND state='running'),
  event 'canceled' detail "forced (was running)". If the row changed
  underneath: outcome raced -> CONFLICT exit 5 reporting the state it
  became.
- done/dead/canceled: outcome conflict -> CONFLICT exit 5 ("already
  <state>"; remediation for dead mentions retry; for canceled says
  nothing to do). Deliberately strict, matching retry-on-queued: no
  idempotent-success shortcut.
- missing: NOT_FOUND exit 1.
Success data: {job_id, state: "canceled", was_running}. When was_running,
add warning KILL_ASYNC: the child dies within roughly one heartbeat
interval, not synchronously.

Worker-side delivery:
- store.update_heartbeat returns the UPDATE's rowcount.
- The Heartbeat thread treats a *successful* UPDATE that matched zero rows
  as proof of lost ownership and fires an on_lost callback; the worker
  then SIGTERMs the child's process group, waits the grace period,
  SIGKILLs (reuse the existing group-kill helper). sqlite exceptions
  (e.g. transient busy) keep the current swallow-and-retry behavior and
  must NOT fire on_lost — only rowcount-0 proof counts.
- The worker's subsequent result recording already discards via the
  ownership-guarded completion path; no change needed there.
- Side benefit to verify: a job force-retried or reaped out from under a
  stalled-but-alive worker now gets its child killed by that worker within
  a heartbeat — active side-effect containment, not just a discarded row.
- Residual, documented: if the worker dies at the same moment as the
  cancel, its orphaned child (own session) may run to completion — the
  same at-least-once orphan caveat that applies to worker SIGKILL. The
  job's state is canceled either way, and the reaper ignores
  non-running rows, so nothing resurrects it.

retry on a canceled job requeues it with a fresh budget, exactly like
dead/failed (the existing retry fall-through permits it once the state
exists — add an explicit test).
acceptance_criteria:
- Exit codes asserted for every rule: queued cancel 0, running unforced 2,
  running forced 0 with KILL_ASYNC warning, raced force 5, terminal
  states 5, unknown id 1.
- Race test (release-gating): cancels racing completing jobs; the final
  state is exactly one of done|canceled and never changes afterward — no
  interleaving yields a canceled row later flipped to done.
- Kill-delivery test (release-gating): a long-running child (which
  touched a marker file at start) has its whole process group dead within
  a test-tuned heartbeat interval of a forced cancel; wait on markers and
  process polling, never fixed sleeps.
- A transiently failing heartbeat (simulated busy) does not kill the
  child.
- retry on canceled requeues with attempts=0; goldens pinned.
close_reason: Shipped in commit 7674326. store.cancel_job (single BEGIN IMMEDIATE, outcomes ok/ok_running/not_found/running_unforced/terminal/raced), cancel verb with every exit rule tested (0/2/0+KILL_ASYNC/5/5/1), update_heartbeat returns rowcount, Heartbeat fires on_lost only on zero-row-successful-UPDATE proof (exceptions swallow+retry, tested), worker kills own child group on lost ownership. Release-gating tests: kill delivery within a heartbeat, force-retry containment, cancel-vs-completion race settles to exactly done|canceled and stays stable. retry-on-canceled requeues attempts=0. envelope-cancel-queued golden + capabilities re-pinned. Suite 186 green, concurrency suite 3x flake-free. Guarded-recording test updated for the new containment behavior.

title: wait verb: block until jobs reach a terminal state
description:
The submit-many-then-wait pattern is what makes a job queue usable as a
parallelism primitive in scripts: add N jobs, wait on the ids, branch on
the exit code. Nothing today blocks on completion. The exit code must
distinguish "the tool failed" from "a job failed" or shell one-liners
cannot branch correctly.
design:
spoolctl wait <id...> [--timeout SECONDS] [--poll-interval SECONDS]
[--db PATH] [--json]
- Upfront existence check on all ids; any missing -> NOT_FOUND exit 1
  listing the missing ids (fail fast beats waiting forever on a typo).
- Poll (default interval 0.5s) until every job is terminal: done, dead,
  or canceled. failed is not a resting state — the failure transaction
  requeues or dead-letters in the same write, so polling never observes a
  settled 'failed'.
- Exit 0 when all done; exit 6 when any dead/canceled. Exit 6 pairs with
  ok:true and empty errors[] in the envelope (documented contract
  exception; consumers use data.all_succeeded).
- One envelope at the end, never partial output: data: {all_succeeded,
  jobs: {"<id>": {state, attempts, last_exit_code, last_error}}}.
- --timeout (float > 0; default none, wait forever): on expiry, error
  TIMEOUT, exit 4 (retryable — remediation shows re-running the same wait).
- Pure reader; a SIGKILLed worker's job surfaces through the reaper as
  eventually done or dead with no special casing here.
acceptance_criteria:
- Exit paths asserted: all-done 0, mixed 6, timeout 4, unknown id 1.
- End-to-end (release-gating): add jobs, start a worker, SIGKILL it
  mid-job, start a second worker; wait returns 0 after reap + rerun.
  Side-effect assertions allow 1-or-2 marker occurrences (at-least-once:
  a killed worker's child can survive and complete before the rerun).
- Golden pinned; identical settled queues give identical data_hash.
close_reason: Shipped in commit 861735c. cmd_wait polls one connection until every id is done/dead/canceled; fail-fast NOT_FOUND lists all missing ids; exits 0/6/4/1 all asserted; exit 6 pairs with ok:true + empty errors (VerbResult.exit_code added to the framework, golden envelope-wait-mixed pins the exception). Determinism asserted via identical data_hash on a settled queue. Release-gating crash test: SIGKILL worker mid-job, second worker reaps and reruns, wait returns 0, marker 1-or-2 lines. Suite 199 green.

title: work --drain: run until the queue settles
description:
Cron and CI want "start a worker, process everything, exit 0" without a
daemon to babysit. "Everything" must mean settled, not "nothing claimable
this instant": a failed job requeued with backoff has next_run_at in the
future, and another worker's in-flight job may still fail and requeue —
draining must wait both out or CI exits with work left behind.
design:
- work --drain; combining with --once is INVALID_INPUT exit 1.
- Loop exactly like normal mode (reap pass -> claim -> execute -> record);
  when a claim pass finds nothing, check the new
  store.unsettled_count(conn) — COUNT(*) of jobs in state queued or
  running — and exit when it is zero; otherwise sleep the poll interval
  and continue.
- Exit 0. With --json, emit one final envelope: data {drained, executed}
  where executed counts jobs this process ran and drained is false when a
  signal stopped the loop early. Without --json, per-job stderr lines as
  in loop mode, nothing on stdout until exit.
- Signal handling identical to loop mode (first signal finishes the
  in-flight job, second kills its process group).
- Semantics note for docs: settled is judged at observation time; a job
  added after the final check belongs to the next invocation.
acceptance_criteria:
- Drain test (release-gating): seed jobs including one that fails into a
  backoff requeue while a second worker holds an in-flight job; drain
  exits only after both settle, exit 0, executed count sane.
- --drain --once rejected with exit 1.
- Golden pinned for the --json envelope.
close_reason: Shipped (work --drain commit, see git log). worker.work_loop gains drain mode returning {drained, executed}; settled test via store.unsettled_count when a claim pass comes up empty; signal semantics unchanged (early stop reports drained:false). Tests: --drain --once exit 1, empty-queue immediate drain, 3-job drain executed=3, in-process backoff wait-out, and the release-gating two-worker test (holder's in-flight sleep + flaky backoff requeue; drain exits 0 only after all done, unsettled_count 0). work-drain-empty golden + capabilities re-pinned. Suite 205 green.

title: prune verb: retention for jobs, events, and output files
description:
Nothing ever deletes finished jobs, their event rows, or their captured
output files — retention was explicitly deferred out of v0.1, and
redirect-to-file capture makes disk growth real. prune deletes old
terminal jobs with age/state filters and reclaims the output directory.
design:
spoolctl prune --older-than DURATION [--state CSV] [--dry-run] [--db PATH]
[--json]
- --older-than is required. DURATION = integer with optional s|m|h|d
  suffix; bare integer means seconds; 0 matches everything. Matching:
  finished_at < now - duration (served by idx_jobs_finished).
- --state: default "done"; allowed values only done, dead, canceled.
  Anything else — including queued, running, failed — is INVALID_INPUT
  exit 1: prune cannot touch non-terminal work by construction.
- Deletion order (files first, rows second):
  1. SELECT matched job ids and all their attempts' output paths.
  2. Best-effort delete each attempt's stdout/stderr files and the
     per-attempt and per-job output directories; tally freed bytes;
     missing files are fine.
  3. One BEGIN IMMEDIATE: re-check each job still matches (state + age —
     a matched dead job may have been retried meanwhile; skip it, its
     files are gone but the output verb already degrades gracefully on
     missing files), then delete its attempts, job_events, and jobs rows.
  A crash between steps 2 and 3 leaves rows whose files are gone;
  re-running prune finishes the job. The reverse order would strand
  invisible orphan files that nothing could ever find again.
- --dry-run performs step 1 only and reports what would be deleted.
- data: {matched, deleted_jobs, deleted_attempts, deleted_events,
  freed_bytes, dry_run}. Zero matches is success (exit 0).
acceptance_criteria:
- Duration parsing asserted (suffixes, bare seconds, 0, garbage -> exit 1).
- --dry-run deletes nothing and reports accurate counts.
- Safety test (release-gating): prune runs concurrently with active
  workers; no queued/running row and no live attempt's output file is
  ever deleted.
- Re-run-after-partial-failure test: delete files, skip row deletion
  (simulated crash), re-run prune, rows gone.
- Golden pinned.
close_reason: Shipped (prune commit, see git log). store.prune_matches (pure reads) + store.prune_delete (BEGIN IMMEDIATE re-check per job, children-first row deletion); cmd_prune deletes files first then rows, tallies freed_bytes, best-effort rmdirs. Duration and state grammars fully tested through the surface (suffixes, garbage, non-terminal states rejected with did_you_mean). dry-run accuracy, partial-failure re-run, retried-job skip, and the deterministic live-worker safety test all green. Suite 219 green, concurrency 3x flake-free.

title: v0.2 release gate: flake-free concurrency suite and pinned contract
description:
The release rides on the concurrency suite: every reliability claim is
backed by a test, and a flaky gate is no gate. v0.2 adds five
release-gating concurrency scenarios (cancel race, kill delivery, drain
settling, wait through worker SIGKILL, prune safety) on top of the v0.1
six; the combined suite must be demonstrably flake-free before tagging.
design:
- Run the full test suite 10 consecutive times locally with zero failures.
- CI green on the full matrix: ubuntu-latest and macos-latest, Python 3.10
  and 3.13.
- Audit that every new verb has a pinned envelope golden and that the
  capabilities golden covers all eleven verbs, exit code 6, and the
  canceled state.
- Confirm the single-file build still packages the new code and its
  --help stays under the terse-help budget.
acceptance_criteria:
- 10/10 clean local full-suite runs recorded in the issue notes.
- CI matrix green on the release candidate commit.
- capabilities --json golden enumerates 11 verbs; every documented exit
  path for the new verbs is asserted somewhere in the suite.
- Single-file artifact builds and runs --help and one add/work/wait
  round-trip.
notes: GATE PASSED at RC commit 7187fbc. 10/10 consecutive clean full-suite runs (219 tests each) on the frozen tree — zero failures. CI matrix (ubuntu/macos x py3.10/3.13) green on 7187fbc. Capabilities golden enumerates 11 verbs, exit codes 0-6, canceled in all three enums; every new verb has a pinned envelope golden; every documented exit path for the new verbs asserted (incl. the defensively-unreachable raced-cancel mapping, pinned via mocked store). Single-file artifact builds, --help under budget, add/work/wait round-trip verified both manually and in test_scaffold. Note: an earlier 10x attempt showed 1 error at run 3 — caused by a concurrent test-file edit during the loop, not flake; rerun clean on the frozen tree.
close_reason: Gate passed at commit 7187fbc: 10/10 clean local full-suite runs (recorded in notes), CI matrix green, capabilities golden covers 11 verbs + exit 6 + canceled, all new-verb exit paths asserted, single-file artifact round-trip verified. v0.2 is release-candidate ready.
